content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Pr;ies Commission
January 7,
1994
Mr. Loren Kaye
California ‘N-tie and Coimnrce Agency
801 K Street, Suite 1700
Sacranento,
CA 95814
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—93-490
Dear Mr. Kaye:
This is in response to your letters dated December 10 and 17,
1993, requesting advice concerning the steps that should be taken
by the Trade and Commerce Agency
(“Agency”) either to return or
disburse approximately $114 , 000 in corporate donations held in a
special bank account established by the Agency.
This response does not evaluate nor provide immunity for any
conduct that has already occurred and is based solely on the facts
that you have presented in your two letters to the Coimnission.
(See In re Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71; Gov’t Code
§ 83114;
2
Cal.
Code Regs.
§ 18329.)
QUESTION
What are the permissible uses, if any,
of funds held in a
special State bank account, that were solicited by the Governor
1
from private corporations for the Governor’ s recent trade mission
to Asia?
ONWSION
Because we do not render advice regarding past conduct, we
cannot give you a definitive answer concerning whether the funds
in the account constitute gifts to the State of California or
gifts to one or more public officials.
If the corporations
donated the funds to the Agency, the funds may be transferred to
the Agency’s revolving fund account to offset expenses of the
trip.
If,
however, the funds were donated to a specific or high
level public official, the funds could constitute illegal gifts in
excess of the statutory ljmit of $270.
In the latter case,
we
would require you, within 30 days of receipt of this letter, to:
1
The term “Governor” as used herein includes Governor Wilson as
well as members of his staff.
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807
•
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—93—490
Page
2
(1) return the entire amount or the part over the limit to the
donors; or
(2) transfer/donate the funds to another State account
or private organization where the funds could not benefit a public
official and not be used to supplant Agency funds.
FACTS
In July 1993, the Governor approved a trade mission to Asia.
A delegation of state officials and private business representa
tives led by Governor Wilson would visit seven cities and conduct
90 meetings in less than three weeks.
Governor Wilson had the
final say in determining who would accompany him and what meetings
would be held on the trip.
The Governor decided in October 1993 that he wanted the
majority of the state officials’ travel costs to be paid for with
private funds.
The Governor asked two individuals outside of
state government to raise the funds from the private sector.
Those who donated funds would be invited to join the Governor as
part of the private—sector delegation.
The Department of Finance instructed the Undersecretary of
the Trade and Commerce Agency to establish a special account in
which the private donations would be placed.
The. Agency made all
travel plans and paid for all travel and accommodations with State
of California funds before knowing who was going and before any
private donations were received.
The Governor sent a solicitation letter to a number of
corporations.
The letter informed them that their donations were
expected to be gifts to the state.
Enclosed was a sample letter
prepared by a private law firm for the corporations to send back
with their check, restricting the funds for expenses associated
with the Governor’s Asian Economic Development trip.
The Depart
ment of Finance required donors to “earmark” the funds in this
manner.
The trade mission occurred in late November and early
December 1993 and was financed with State funds.
To date,
none of
the approximately $114,000 has been disbursed from the special
account.
ANALYSIS
The $114,000 in the special account is comprised of an
unknown number of separate donations from private corporations.
The first issue that must be resolved is whether the money in the
bank account is a “gift.”
The Political Reform Act
2 defines a
“gift” as “any payment to the extent that consideration of equal
or greater value is not received
....“
(Gov’t Code § 82028.)
A
“payment” is “a payment, distribution, transfer,
loan,
advance,
2
Gov’t Code § 81000—91015.


File No.
A—93--490
Page
3
deposit, gift or other rendering of money, property, services or
anything else of value, whether tangible or intangible. “
(Gov’t
Code § 82044.)
V
V
Airfare to a foreign country,
accommodations in that country,
and related travel expenses constitute items of value,
and we as
sume that the mem1ers of the state delegation did not provide
consideration of equal value for these payments.
Thus, the
corporate donations are “gifts” under the Act.
Elected state officials, members of state boards,
and
designated state employees generally may not accept gifts with a
total value of more than $270 in calendar year 1993 from a single
sdurce.
(Gov’t Code SS 89504—05;
2 Cal.
Code Regs.
§S 18940.1,
18954.)
The 10 to 15 state officials who you indicate went on the
trade mission would exceed this limit if any of them received a
gift attributable to any donor that exceeded this ainoimt.
Thus,
the critical issue is whether the corporate donations are:
(1)
prohibited gifts to Governor Wilson and/or other state officials
who attended the mission,
or
(2) gifts to the Trade and Commerce
Agency,
which are permissible.
REQUIREMEPS OF THE STONE OPINION
ma formal opinion issued in 1977,
the Commission stated
that:
V
“There may be some situations
...
where
V
surrounding circumstances show that the gift
was made to the
[governmental entity]
only,
without providing any significant or unusual
benefit to the official.”
(In re Stone
(1977)
3
FPPC Ops.
52,
56
(emphasis added).)
Such payments are not reportable gifts,
because the Commission
deems them to be a substitute for expense reimbursement that the
governmental entity would otherwise have to pay the officials.
The Commission in Stone warned that “no immutable guidelines”
could be devised for deciding when these types of gifts occur.
It
then set forth four minimum criteria that must be met before it is
presumed that a donor is giving a gift to a governmental agency
only and not to a public official who benefits from it.
First, the donor must intend to donate the gift to the
.crovernment and not to an official.
As noted above, the Governor
in the present case attached a form letter to the solicitation and
instructed the potential donors to return a similar letter with
their money.
You did not provide us with the cover letters, if
any, that the donors returned with their gifts.
Such letters
normally would be probative concerning whether the donor intended
to give an unrestricted gift to a government agency or whether the
donor earmarked the gift for a particular individual or purpose.


File No.
A-93-490
Page
4
However,
a cover letter containing “boilerplate” language
supplied by the soliôiting individual or agency might be entitled
to less weight, especially if other .evidence led to a different
conclusion regarding the true intentions of the donor.
You imply
that the cover letters in the instant case are relevant and have
raised the issue whether Stone allows earmarking for a specific
public official or for a specific purpose.
Therefore,
we thought
it appropriate to coimnent on the permissibility of earmarking
under Stone.
You seem to take alternative positions in your letter with
regard to earmarking of the funds for a particular official by the
donors.
Initially,
you indicate that “[tihe donors did not intend
[the money] to benefit
-- nor even know the identity of
—- any
particular state officials.”
On the other hand,
you argue that earmarking for a particular
official is permissible.
You cite three advice letters issued by
the Commission which you believe have sanctioned earmarking of
gifts for specific officials in the past.
3
We disagree.
The Raye letter
(A—84-077
[Apr.
5,
1984]),
is inapposite
because it merely sanctioned the donation of a tangible item,
a
house, that could
be’ used not only by the sitting Governor,
but by
future Governors as well.
“[I]t is significant that the use of
the house is not limited to Governor Deuicmejian and his family; it
may be used for future Governors, at the discretion of the State.”
(.I at p.
2.)
The letter sanctioned the donation of tangible
property earmarked by the donor for a specified purpose, e.g.,
a
museum or public university.
The letter stated that such gifts
did not have to meet all of the Stone criteria.
Nothing in the
opinion sanctions the earmarking of travel, hotel accommodations,
and meals for a particular public official.
The Iiubrecht letter
(A-83-l75
[July 21,
1983]),
asked the
question whether an association could “pay for the cost of having
appropriate representatives of the Energy Commission and the Air
Resources Board attend the ceremony in Detroit to speak and to
formally accept the first methanol bus for the state’s fleet.”
(Id. at p.
1.)
Our answer was that “if the Association donates
the gift of transportation costs to the Energy Commission and to
the Air Resources Board”
and follow the other criteria of the
You also contend that a draft regulation of the Commission
sanctions such earmarking.
Draft regulations lend very little
guidance in determining the intent of the Commission.
(Cf. Bell
v.
Department of Motor Vehicles
(1992)
11 Cal.App.4th 304,
313
[“The light shed by such unadopted proposals is too dim to pierce
statutory obscurities.
As evidence of legislative intent they
have little value.”].)


•
File No.
A—93—490
Page
5
Stone opinion, it would be permissible. ( at P.
3.)
That let—
ter does not allow a donor to earmark a gift for a specific of
ficial.
4
The Koppes advice letter
(A-92-582
[Dec.
2,
1992]), that you
cite is even clearer in not permitting earmarking of gifts for
particular officias.
It states that “a gift to a public agency
should not be restricted for the use of identifiable individuals,
but rather be made available at the discretion of the agency.”
In
early 1992, the Public Employees’ Retirement System
(“PERS”)
legal
unit submitted to the Commission two draft written policy state
ment containing guidelines for when board members and staff could
accept payments from third parties for travel,
lodging,
and meals.
We issued an informal advice letter
(1—92—392
[July 23,
1992])
commenting on those provisions and also memorialized therein oral
advice that Stone predated limits on the amount of gifts and was
not intended to permit public officials to circumvent these limits
by using their agency as an intermediary.
PERS General Counsel, Richard Koppes,
then wrote a follow-up
request for formal advice,
in which he asked whether it is enough
to satisfy Stone (specifically, criterion number three), if the
donor does not expressly restrict the use of a gift to specified
or high-level employees.
Mr.
Koppes noted that the Commission had
never stated whether an actual pattern of use by such high—level
officials could put donors on notice that their gift was earmarked
and not actually unrestricted as required by the Stone opinion.
Our response, in allowing the use of Stone, relies heavily on
the fact that a written resolution of PERS provided that the use
of such gifts would not be limited to any specified high—level
officials and would be used for expenses that PERS would routinely
incur in the normal course of business.
The Koppes advice letter
states that the gifts would meet the Stone criteria; thus,
one may
infer that the fact that high level officials later benefited from
such a gift would not necessarily affect that result.
5
This is not to say that a donor has to ignore reality.
If a
corporation makes its helicopter available for a county to send a
person to inspect possible sites for a new landfill, the donor can
meet the requirements of Stone,
even if it is probable that the
county will send a particular official,
such as the Director of
Planning.
Stone does not,
however, permit a donor to earmark a
gift for a specific public official under any circumstances.
A
gift to a particular public official is always reportable and
subject to the applicable limit.
Stone merely provides a way for
donors to make bona fide gifts to public agencies, without any
incidental benefit to a public official being reportable by him or
her.
(See, e.g.,
Koppes advice letter [discussed below].)
There will be exceptions.
For example, if the same high-level
public official and his wife have taken a trip for three
consecutive years paid for by the same donor,
those facts would


•
File No.
A—93—490
Page
6
It is not in dispute that donors may earmark gifts for a
specific governmental purpose.
In the present case, if the donors
specified that their funds were to be used only to send a state
delegation on a trade mission to the Far East, that earmarking
would not run afoul of Stone’s requirements.
The second criterion, in Stone provides that the governmental
agency must exercise substantial control over use of the gift.
Your letter contends that the “agency” for these purposes was the
Governor’s Administration,
and Governor Wilson has the authority
to decide who will represent that administration on official
trips’.
This is undoubtedly true.
Nevertheless, in the context of
accepting large gifts of money, if an agency headed by a single
individual is seeking to invoke the Stone opinion,
common sense
would dictate that the agency head delegate control of the funds
and the selection of gift recipients to another person who would
not benefit from the gift.
If possible, this person should be
independent of the control of the head of the agency.
The third criterion of Stone is:
“The donor has not limited use’ of the
gift to specified or high level employees,
but rather has made it available to
[agency]
personnel
... without regard to official status.”
This point was covered earlier in the discussion of earmarking.
6
For example,
a developer could not earmark a gift of airfare,
ac—
conimodations,
and tickets to the Olympics for the mayor of San
Jose,
and comply with this part of Stone.
The issue in the present case is whether the corporate donors
limited the use of their funds to high level officials in the
Governor’s administration.
Again,
we cannot advise you concerning
past conduct.
Nevertheless,
as discussed above, if the Agency,
and not the donor,
made the decision as to who would receive the
funds, this part of the test is met.
7
tend to show that the donor did not meet the first or third
criteria of Stone.
6
Requirements one and three of Stone deal with actions within
the control of the donor
(intended recipient and earmarking),
whereas two and four are actions the agency must take (control
gift and formalizing the gift in writing).
However,
under certain circumstances, that may beg the question
as to whether the other criteria were met.
If a single official
controlled distribution of,
and benefited from,
a donor’s gift,
one could conclude that the donor intended to give the money to
that official and not to the government.


—
-
File No.
A—93--490
Page
7
Finally, criterion number four requires that “ftlhe makirg
and use of the gift was formalized in a resolution of the
ragency]
(a written public record will suffice for administrative agencies
not possessing the legislative power of adopting resolutions)
which embodies the standards.set forth above.”
(Emphasis added.)
Drafting such a document is important not only to make a public
record of the otherwise unreported gift to the agency, but also to
force the agency to think through its policy with regard to the
acceptance and use of such gifts.
You did not attach your general
written policy with regard to such gifts (if you have one)
or a
record of these specific corporate contributions; thus,
you should
review whether you met this part of the Stone test.
PERMISSIBLE USES OF ThE FUNDS
As indicated above, the Legal Division of this agency does
not comment on past conduct.
This is one of the ways that we at
tempt to keep the advice function of the agency separate from the
enforcement function.
Therefore,
we cannot answer your first
question,
namely,
whether the funds in the special account were
“gifts to the State.”
We trust that the foregoing discussion of
Stone’s requirements will assist you in making that decision.
If
the funds met the requirements of Stone,
8 then you may reimburse
the Trade and Commerce Agency’s revolving fund account for
expenses associated with the trade mission.
If,
on the other hand,
you determine that the gifts were not
raised in accordance with the Stone opinion,
you may:
(l)
return
the funds to the donors; or
(2)
donate or transfer the funds to
any account, public or private, that could not be used to supplant
other State funds used to pay for the trade mission or benefit a
public official.
For example,
you ask whether the gifts from the
corporate donors could be transferred to the California Export
Promotion Account or some other Trade and Commerce account for
other Agency activities.
The problem we see with that approach is
that the funds could replace other monies that could be used for
the Governor’s trade mission.
There is less chance of this with
transfer to the State’s General Fund or to a special fund where
uses are restricted by law.
You also ask about transfer to a nonprofit organization such
as the California Economic Development Corporation
(“CEDC”).
The
same analysis applies.
If there is any possibility that the
CEDC
would sponsor a future reception,
or pay for travel that would
otherwise be paid for by Trade and Commerce, then the corporate
gifts would enable the Agency to save funds it otherwise would
have used.
Such an indirect use of gifts over the limit would be
no more permissible than a direct use.
8
We have not found any previous advice of this agency that al
lowed an agency to comply with factors two and four of Stone after
a gift was accepted.


•
File No.
A-93--490
Page
8
Obviously,
we would prefer to give this type of advice
earlier in the process.
Nevertheless,
we can say without
hesitation that it is perfectly legal for the Trade and Commerce
Agency to solicit private donations in excess of the gift limits
for foreign travel of state officials, as long as the donors and
the Agency carefully follow the procedures and meet the tests
discussed above.
If you have any questions or need further information, please
do not hesitate to contact me.
Sincerely,
(ZAJ€
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel

and not the donor,
made the decision as to who would receive the
funds, this part of the test is met.
7
tend to show that the donor did not meet the first or third
criteria of Stone.

ragency]

or a

you should
review whether you met this part of the Stone test.

not comment on past conduct.

tempt to keep the advice function of the agency separate from the
enforcement function.
Therefore,
we cannot answer your first
question,
namely,

“gifts to the State.”

the funds met the requirements of Stone,
8 then you may reimburse
the Trade and Commerce Agency’s revolving fund account for
expenses associated with the trade mission.

on the other hand,

raised in accordance with the Stone opinion,
you may:
(l)
return
the funds to the donors; or
(2)
donate or transfer the funds to
any account, public or private, that could not be used to supplant
other State funds used to pay for the trade mission or benefit a
public official.
For example,
you ask whether the gifts from the
corporate donors could be transferred to the California Export
Promotion Account or some other Trade and Commerce account for
other Agency activities.
The problem we see with that approach is
that the funds could replace other monies that could be used for
the Governor’s trade mission.
There is less chance of this with
transfer to the State’s General Fund or to a special fund where
uses are restricted by law.
You also ask about transfer to a nonprofit organization such
as the California Economic Development Corporation
(“CEDC”).
The
same analysis applies.
If there is any possibility that the
CEDC
would sponsor a future reception,
or pay for travel that would
otherwise be paid for by Trade and Commerce, then the corporate
gifts would enable the Agency to save funds it otherwise would
have used.
Such an indirect use of gifts over the limit would be
no more permissible than a direct use.
8
We have not found any previous advice of this agency that al
lowed an agency to comply with factors two and four of Stone after
a gift was accepted.


•
File No.
A-93--490
Obviously,
we would prefer to give this type of advice
earlier in the process.
Nevertheless,
we can say without
hesitation that it is perfectly legal for the Trade and Commerce
Agency to solicit private donations in excess of the gift limits
for foreign travel of state officials, as long as the donors and
the Agency carefully follow the procedures and meet the tests
discussed above.
sections.facts:
In July 1993, the Governor approved a trade mission to Asia.
A delegation of state officials and private business representa
tives led by Governor Wilson would visit seven cities and conduct
90 meetings in less than three weeks.
Governor Wilson had the
final say in determining who would accompany him and what meetings
would be held on the trip.
The Governor decided in October 1993 that he wanted the
majority of the state officials’ travel costs to be paid for with
private funds.
The Governor asked two individuals outside of
state government to raise the funds from the private sector.
Those who donated funds would be invited to join the Governor as
part of the private—sector delegation.
The Department of Finance instructed the Undersecretary of
the Trade and Commerce Agency to establish a special account in
which the private donations would be placed.
The. Agency made all
travel plans and paid for all travel and accommodations with State
of California funds before knowing who was going and before any
private donations were received.
The Governor sent a solicitation letter to a number of
corporations.
The letter informed them that their donations were
expected to be gifts to the state.
Enclosed was a sample letter
prepared by a private law firm for the corporations to send back
with their check, restricting the funds for expenses associated
with the Governor’s Asian Economic Development trip.
The Depart
ment of Finance required donors to “earmark” the funds in this
manner.
The trade mission occurred in late November and early
December 1993 and was financed with State funds.
To date,
none of
the approximately $114,000 has been disbursed from the special
account.
sections.analysis:
The $114,000 in the special account is comprised of an
unknown number of separate donations from private corporations.
The first issue that must be resolved is whether the money in the
bank account is a “gift.”
The Political Reform Act
2 defines a
“gift” as “any payment to the extent that consideration of equal
or greater value is not received
....“
(Gov’t Code § 82028.)
A
“payment” is “a payment, distribution, transfer,
loan,
advance,
2
Gov’t Code § 81000—91015.


File No.
A—93--490
deposit, gift or other rendering of money, property, services or
anything else of value, whether tangible or intangible. “
(Gov’t
Code § 82044.)
V
V
Airfare to a foreign country,
accommodations in that country,
and related travel expenses constitute items of value,
and we as
sume that the mem1ers of the state delegation did not provide
consideration of equal value for these payments.
Thus, the
corporate donations are “gifts” under the Act.
Elected state officials, members of state boards,
and
designated state employees generally may not accept gifts with a
total value of more than $270 in calendar year 1993 from a single
sdurce.
(Gov’t Code SS 89504—05;
2 Cal.
Code Regs.
§S 18940.1,
18954.)
The 10 to 15 state officials who you indicate went on the
trade mission would exceed this limit if any of them received a
gift attributable to any donor that exceeded this ainoimt.
Thus,
the critical issue is whether the corporate donations are:
(1)
prohibited gifts to Governor Wilson and/or other state officials
who attended the mission,
or
(2) gifts to the Trade and Commerce
Agency,
which are permissible.
REQUIREMEPS OF THE STONE OPINION
ma formal opinion issued in 1977,
the Commission stated
that:
V
“There may be some situations
...
where
V
surrounding circumstances show that the gift
was made to the
[governmental entity]
only,
without providing any significant or unusual
benefit to the official.”
(In re Stone
(1977)
3
FPPC Ops.
52,
56
(emphasis added).)
Such payments are not reportable gifts,
because the Commission
deems them to be a substitute for expense reimbursement that the
governmental entity would otherwise have to pay the officials.
The Commission in Stone warned that “no immutable guidelines”
could be devised for deciding when these types of gifts occur.
It
then set forth four minimum criteria that must be met before it is
presumed that a donor is giving a gift to a governmental agency
only and not to a public official who benefits from it.
First, the donor must intend to donate the gift to the
.crovernment and not to an official.
As noted above, the Governor
in the present case attached a form letter to the solicitation and
instructed the potential donors to return a similar letter with
their money.
You did not provide us with the cover letters, if
any, that the donors returned with their gifts.
Such letters
normally would be probative concerning whether the donor intended
to give an unrestricted gift to a government agency or whether the
donor earmarked the gift for a particular individual or purpose.
However,
a cover letter containing “boilerplate” language
supplied by the soliôiting individual or agency might be entitled
to less weight, especially if other .evidence led to a different

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are the permissible uses, if any,
of funds held in a
special State bank account, that were solicited by the Governor
1
from private corporations for the Governor’ s recent trade mission
to Asia?
ONWSION
Because we do not render advice regarding past conduct, we
cannot give you a definitive answer concerning whether the funds
in the account constitute gifts to the State of California or
gifts to one or more public officials.
If the corporations
donated the funds to the Agency, the funds may be transferred to
the Agency’s revolving fund account to offset expenses of the
trip.
If,
however, the funds were donated to a specific or high
level public official, the funds could constitute illegal gifts in
excess of the statutory ljmit of $270.
In the latter case,
we
would require you, within 30 days of receipt of this letter, to:
1
The term “Governor” as used herein includes Governor Wilson as
well as members of his staff.

•
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—93—490
(1) return the entire amount or the part over the limit to the
donors; or
(2) transfer/donate the funds to another State account
or private organization where the funds could not benefit a public
official and not be used to supplant Agency funds.

CONCLUSION: regarding the true intentions of the donor.
You imply
that the cover letters in the instant case are relevant and have
raised the issue whether Stone allows earmarking for a specific
public official or for a specific purpose.
Therefore,
we thought
it appropriate to coimnent on the permissibility of earmarking
under Stone.
You seem to take alternative positions in your letter with
regard to earmarking of the funds for a particular official by the
donors.
Initially,
you indicate that “[tihe donors did not intend
[the money] to benefit
-- nor even know the identity of
—- any
particular state officials.”
On the other hand,
you argue that earmarking for a particular
official is permissible.
You cite three advice letters issued by
the Commission which you believe have sanctioned earmarking of
gifts for specific officials in the past.
3
We disagree.
The Raye letter
(A—84-077
[Apr.
5,
1984]),
is inapposite
because it merely sanctioned the donation of a tangible item,
a
house, that could
be’ used not only by the sitting Governor,
but by
future Governors as well.
“[I]t is significant that the use of
the house is not limited to Governor Deuicmejian and his family; it
may be used for future Governors, at the discretion of the State.”
(.I at p.
2.)
The letter sanctioned the donation of tangible
property earmarked by the donor for a specified purpose, e.g.,
a
museum or public university.
The letter stated that such gifts
did not have to meet all of the Stone criteria.
Nothing in the
opinion sanctions the earmarking of travel, hotel accommodations,
and meals for a particular public official.
The Iiubrecht letter
(A-83-l75
[July 21,
1983]),
asked the
question whether an association could “pay for the cost of having
appropriate representatives of the Energy Commission and the Air
Resources Board attend the ceremony in Detroit to speak and to
formally accept the first methanol bus for the state’s fleet.”
(Id. at p.
1.)
Our answer was that “if the Association donates
the gift of transportation costs to the Energy Commission and to
the Air Resources Board”
and follow the other criteria of the
You also contend that a draft regulation of the Commission
sanctions such earmarking.
Draft regulations lend very little
guidance in determining the intent of the Commission.
(Cf. Bell
v.
Department of Motor Vehicles
(1992)
11 Cal.App.4th 304,
313
[“The light shed by such unadopted proposals is too dim to pierce
statutory obscurities.
As evidence of legislative intent they
have little value.”].)


•
File No.
A—93—490
Stone opinion, it would be permissible. ( at P.
3.)
That let—
ter does not allow a donor to earmark a gift for a specific of
ficial.
4
The Koppes advice letter
(A-92-582
[Dec.
2,
1992]), that you
cite is even clearer in not permitting earmarking of gifts for
particular officias.
It states that “a gift to a public agency
should not be restricted for the use of identifiable individuals,
but rather be made available at the discretion of the agency.”
In
early 1992, the Public Employees’ Retirement System
(“PERS”)
legal
unit submitted to the Commission two draft written policy state
ment containing guidelines for when board members and staff could
accept payments from third parties for travel,
lodging,
and meals.
We issued an informal advice letter
(1—92—392
[July 23,
1992])
commenting on those provisions and also memorialized therein oral
advice that Stone predated limits on the amount of gifts and was
not intended to permit public officials to circumvent these limits
by using their agency as an intermediary.
PERS General Counsel, Richard Koppes,
then wrote a follow-up
request for formal advice,
in which he asked whether it is enough
to satisfy Stone (specifically, criterion number three), if the
donor does not expressly restrict the use of a gift to specified
or high-level employees.
Mr.
Koppes noted that the Commission had
never stated whether an actual pattern of use by such high—level
officials could put donors on notice that their gift was earmarked
and not actually unrestricted as required by the Stone opinion.
Our response, in allowing the use of Stone, relies heavily on
the fact that a written resolution of PERS provided that the use
of such gifts would not be limited to any specified high—level
officials and would be used for expenses that PERS would routinely
incur in the normal course of business.
The Koppes advice letter
states that the gifts would meet the Stone criteria; thus,
one may
infer that the fact that high level officials later benefited from
such a gift would not necessarily affect that result.
5
This is not to say that a donor has to ignore reality.
If a
corporation makes its helicopter available for a county to send a
person to inspect possible sites for a new landfill, the donor can
meet the requirements of Stone,
even if it is probable that the
county will send a particular official,
such as the Director of
Planning.
Stone does not,
however, permit a donor to earmark a
gift for a specific public official under any circumstances.
A
gift to a particular public official is always reportable and
subject to the applicable limit.
Stone merely provides a way for
donors to make bona fide gifts to public agencies, without any
incidental benefit to a public official being reportable by him or
her.
(See, e.g.,
Koppes advice letter [discussed below].)
There will be exceptions.
For example, if the same high-level
public official and his wife have taken a trip for three
consecutive years paid for by the same donor,
those facts would


•
File No.
A—93—490
It is not in dispute that donors may earmark gifts for a
specific governmental purpose.
In the present case, if the donors
specified that their funds were to be used only to send a state
delegation on a trade mission to the Far East, that earmarking
would not run afoul of Stone’s requirements.
The second criterion, in Stone provides that the governmental
agency must exercise substantial control over use of the gift.
Your letter contends that the “agency” for these purposes was the
Governor’s Administration,
and Governor Wilson has the authority
to decide who will represent that administration on official
trips’.
This is undoubtedly true.
Nevertheless, in the context of
accepting large gifts of money, if an agency headed by a single
individual is seeking to invoke the Stone opinion,
common sense
would dictate that the agency head delegate control of the funds
and the selection of gift recipients to another person who would
not benefit from the gift.
If possible, this person should be
independent of the control of the head of the agency.
The third criterion of Stone is:
“The donor has not limited use’ of the
gift to specified or high level employees,
but rather has made it available to
[agency]
personnel
... without regard to official status.”
This point was covered earlier in the discussion of earmarking.
6
For example,
a developer could not earmark a gift of airfare,
ac—
conimodations,
and tickets to the Olympics for the mayor of San
Jose,
and comply with this part of Stone.
The issue in the present case is whether the corporate donors
limited the use of their funds to high level officials in the
Governor’s administration.
Again,
we cannot advise you concerning
past conduct.
Nevertheless,
as discussed above, if the Agency,
and not the donor,
made the decision as to who would receive the
funds, this part of the test is met.
7
tend to show that the donor did not meet the first or third
criteria of Stone.
6
Requirements one and three of Stone deal with actions within
the control of the donor
(intended recipient and earmarking),
whereas two and four are actions the agency must take (control
gift and formalizing the gift in writing).
However,
under certain circumstances, that may beg the question
as to whether the other criteria were met.
If a single official
controlled distribution of,
and benefited from,
a donor’s gift,
one could conclude that the donor intended to give the money to
that official and not to the government.


—
-
File No.
A—93--490
Finally, criterion number four requires that “ftlhe makirg
and use of the gift was formalized in a resolution of the
ragency]
(a written public record will suffice for administrative agencies
not possessing the legislative power of adopting resolutions)
which embodies the standards.set forth above.”
(Emphasis added.)
Drafting such a document is important not only to make a public
record of the otherwise unreported gift to the agency, but also to
force the agency to think through its policy with regard to the
acceptance and use of such gifts.
You did not attach your general
written policy with regard to such gifts (if you have one)
or a
record of these specific corporate contributions; thus,
you should
review whether you met this part of the Stone test.
PERMISSIBLE USES OF ThE FUNDS
As indicated above, the Legal Division of this agency does
not comment on past conduct.
This is one of the ways that we at
tempt to keep the advice function of the agency separate from the
enforcement function.
Therefore,
we cannot answer your first
question,
namely,
whether the funds in the special account were
“gifts to the State.”
We trust that the foregoing discussion of
Stone’s requirements will assist you in making that decision.
If
the funds met the requirements of Stone,
8 then you may reimburse
the Trade and Commerce Agency’s revolving fund account for
expenses associated with the trade mission.
If,
on the other hand,
you determine that the gifts were not
raised in accordance with the Stone opinion,
you may:
(l)
return
the funds to the donors; or
(2)
donate or transfer the funds to
any account, public or private, that could not be used to supplant
other State funds used to pay for the trade mission or benefit a
public official.
For example,
you ask whether the gifts from the
corporate donors could be transferred to the California Export
Promotion Account or some other Trade and Commerce account for
other Agency activities.
The problem we see with that approach is
that the funds could replace other monies that could be used for
the Governor’s trade mission.
There is less chance of this with
transfer to the State’s General Fund or to a special fund where
uses are restricted by law.
You also ask about transfer to a nonprofit organization such
as the California Economic Development Corporation
(“CEDC”).
The
same analysis applies.
If there is any possibility that the
CEDC
would sponsor a future reception,
or pay for travel that would
otherwise be paid for by Trade and Commerce, then the corporate
gifts would enable the Agency to save funds it otherwise would
have used.
Such an indirect use of gifts over the limit would be
no more permissible than a direct use.
8
We have not found any previous advice of this agency that al
lowed an agency to comply with factors two and four of Stone after
a gift was accepted.


•
File No.
A-93--490
Obviously,
we would prefer to give this type of advice
earlier in the process.
Nevertheless,
we can say without
hesitation that it is perfectly legal for the Trade and Commerce
Agency to solicit private donations in excess of the gift limits
for foreign travel of state officials, as long as the donors and
the Agency carefully follow the procedures and meet the tests
discussed above.

This response does not evaluate nor provide immunity for any conduct that has already occurred and is based solely on the facts that you have presented in your two letters to the Coimnission. (See In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71; Gov’t Code § 83114; 2 Cal. Code Regs. § 18329.) QUESTION What are the permissible uses, if any, of funds held in a special State bank account, that were solicited by the Governor 1 from private corporations for the Governor’ s recent trade mission to Asia? ONWSION Because we do not render advice regarding past conduct, we cannot give you a definitive answer concerning whether the funds in the account constitute gifts to the State of California or gifts to one or more public officials. If the corporations donated the funds to the Agency, the funds may be transferred to the Agency’s revolving fund account to offset expenses of the trip. If, however, the funds were donated to a specific or high level public official, the funds could constitute illegal gifts in excess of the statutory ljmit of $270. In the latter case, we would require you, within 30 days of receipt of this letter, to: 1 The term “Governor” as used herein includes Governor Wilson as well as members of his staff. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 File No. A—93—490 Page 2 (1) return the entire amount or the part over the limit to the donors; or (2) transfer/donate the funds to another State account or private organization where the funds could not benefit a public official and not be used to supplant Agency funds. FACTS In July 1993, the Governor approved a trade mission to Asia. A delegation of state officials and private business representa tives led by Governor Wilson would visit seven cities and conduct 90 meetings in less than three weeks. Governor Wilson had the final say in determining who would accompany him and what meetings would be held on the trip. The Governor decided in October 1993 that he wanted the majority of the state officials’ travel costs to be paid for with private funds. The Governor asked two individuals outside of state government to raise the funds from the private sector. Those who donated funds would be invited to join the Governor as part of the private—sector delegation.
